ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for gyudon (beef donburi). FOODON_03311737 (beef dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (beef meat dishes) is the closest parent; no specific gyudon code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for gyudon or beef donburi identified.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of beef don is approximately 400 g, comprising roughly 200 g of steamed white rice topped with approximately 150 g of simmered beef and onion mixture and 50 g of sauce and broth.

nutrient_source.title: Gyudon (beef bowl) — composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked white rice (FDC ID 168878), cooked beef (FDC ID 174032), and soy-sauce-based seasoning components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry for gyudon as a composite dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: cooked short-grain white rice (~58% of dish by weight), simmered thinly sliced beef (~37%), and soy-sauce/mirin/onion seasoning (~5%). Macros weighted by proportion. Sodium elevated due to soy sauce seasoning. Values represent a typical restaurant-style gyudon at approximately 400 g per serving.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mixed rice-and-meat dishes; white rice GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for gyudon exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 54 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: Japanese short-grain white rice has a published GI of approximately 48–72 depending on cooking method; mixed rice-and-protein dishes consistently show GI reduction relative to plain rice due to protein and fat co-ingestion. A composite GI of 54 is defensible as a conservative low estimate for a beef-and-rice bowl, consistent with analogous mixed rice dishes in Atkinson et al. 2021. GL per serving = (54 × 74 g available carbohydrate per 400 g serving) / 100 = 40.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A single 400 g serving of beef don provides approximately 1520 mg sodium, representing over 65% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-soy-sauce preparation or limit portion size to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from white rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 54 and a glycemic load of approximately 40 per standard serving, beef don poses a moderate postprandial glucose challenge; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-rice portion or substitute with brown rice to reduce glycemic impact, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and elevated sodium from soy sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Beef don contains approximately 6.4 g saturated fat and 1520 mg sodium per standard serving; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption, choose leaner beef cuts when possible, and request lower-sodium seasoning to reduce atherogenic and hypertensive risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Don, known in Japanese as Gyudon (牛丼), is a classic Japanese donburi (rice bowl) dish consisting of a generous portion of steamed short-grain white rice topped with thinly sliced beef and onion simmered in a sweet-savoury sauce made from dashi broth, soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar. The dish originated in Japan in the late 19th century and is one of the most popular fast-food and casual-dining staples in Japanese cuisine, served at chains such as Yoshinoya, Sukiya, and Matsuya. The beef is typically sliced very thin, allowing it to absorb the umami-rich braising liquid quickly, resulting in tender, juicy meat with a glossy, slightly sweet glaze. The texture contrast between the soft, sticky rice and the silky braised beef is a defining characteristic. In Singapore, gyudon is served at Japanese restaurants and food delivery platforms, often with optional toppings such as a soft-poached egg (onsen tamago), pickled ginger (beni shoga), or shredded nori. Nutritionally, a standard 400 g serving provides approximately 592 kcal, 34 g protein, 16.8 g fat, and 74 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is elevated at approximately 1520 mg per serving due to soy sauce seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load is moderate to high per serving, primarily driven by the white rice base. The dish is a good source of iron, zinc, vitamin B12, and niacin from the beef component.
common_user_queries: How many calories in beef don?
What is in a Japanese beef rice bowl?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry for gyudon as a composite dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked white rice, cooked beef, and soy-sauce-based seasoning, weighted by typical dish proportions. GI is an analogous estimate based on mixed rice-and-protein dish data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this composite dish. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this composite dish. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this composite dish. The food_name has been normalised to 'Beef Don (Gyudon)' as the canonical English name; the input name '503D Beef Don (D)' appears to be a restaurant menu code and is preserved in aliases. The dish is not confirmed halal as standard gyudon uses mirin and sake (alcohol-containing ingredients); halal status depends on restaurant preparation. GI category is 'low' (GI = 54, which is ≤ 55 per Veritas threshold rules). GL per serving calculated as (54 × 74) / 100 = 40.0 g for a 400 g serving containing approximately 74 g available carbohydrate.
